slides.title: Best Practices for Public Networks
slides.subtitle: Three wireless attacks, three defenses, and why instinct alone is not enough
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Lesson 1.3, Best Practices for Public Networks, Day 1 of 4. Today is the foundation day. By the time you leave, you will know three specific wireless attacks the AP exam tests by name, three CB-required defenses, and two things that most people believe about public Wi-Fi that are flat-out wrong. Days 2 and 3 are application. Day 4 is the quiz. Let's go.

slides.prompt: You're at a coffee shop. Three networks show up: CoffeeShop_Guest · CoffeeShop Free · FreeCoffeeShop-5G. Which one do you connect to, and why?
slides.subprompt: After you pick one: what information did you actually use to make that decision? Is that enough to keep you safe?
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Five minutes. Pick one of those three networks and write down your reasoning. I'm not looking for a right answer right now, I'm looking for your instinct. What made you choose that one? Was it the name? The signal strength? A gut feeling? Most people connect to public Wi-Fi using exactly the kind of reasoning you just wrote down. By the end of this lesson, you'll know exactly what an attacker exploits about that reasoning.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes packet has 6 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: Adversary Classification
sections.description: Low-skilled vs high-skilled adversaries, plus six motivations. All CB-required for LO 1.3.A.

sections.title: Three Wireless Attacks
sections.description: Evil twin, jamming/DoS, and war driving. All three are CB-specified, high-frequency exam items (LO 1.3.B).

sections.title: Three CB Defenses
sections.description: SSID verification, VPN use, and protocol-encryption awareness. Know all three and their limitations (LO 1.3.C).

sections.title: Attack-Defense Mapping
sections.description: Connect each CB attack to the CB defense that stops it. The AP exam loves to test this mapping.

sections.title: Enrichment: Active vs Passive
sections.description: MITM vs packet sniffing. Not directly CB-required, but it helps you reason through complex scenarios.

sections.title: Stop and Think
sections.description: Two full-sentence written responses before you leave. Maps directly to LO 1.3.B and LO 1.3.C.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 1.3 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Take a quick look at your Guided Notes packet. Six sections today. Green sections are CB-required and will appear on the AP exam. Blue is enrichment. Start with sections 1 through 4 on the CB Standard path, or all six if you are in the Deep Dive track. Keep your pencil moving throughout the video and lecture. Section 5 is the Stop and Think, your two written responses. I collect those at the end of class.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Identify the type of adversary conducting a cyberattack (LO 1.3.A)
Describe actions individuals can take to increase protection of sensitive data when using the internet and Wi-Fi (LO 1.3.C)

slides.script: Three learning objectives, three CB verbs: identify, identify, describe. These map directly to what the AP exam will ask. LO 1.3.A is adversary classification. LO 1.3.B is the three wireless attacks. LO 1.3.C is the three defenses. Notice that all three LOs are skill 1 and skill 2 in the CB framework. That means you need to be able to recognize an attack from a description AND explain how a defense reduces risk. Both will appear on the exam.

slides.label: Who's Behind the Attack?
slides.script: Section one. Adversary classification. This is LO 1.3.A. The CB framework wants you to be able to look at a cyberattack and identify the type of adversary who carried it out, based on the tools they used, the vulnerabilities they exploited, and what motivated them. Seven minutes here.

slides.heading: Adversary Skill Levels
slides.subheading: The CB framework classifies adversaries by skill. Skill level determines which attacks they can carry out.

left.title: Low-Skilled
left.definition: Relies on tools created by others
examples: Purchases pre-built malware or exploit kits online
Cannot adapt tools when defenses are updated
Also called a script kiddie in informal usage

right.title: High-Skilled
right.definition: Can build and modify their own attack tools
examples: Creates new malware or modifies existing tools
Can discover zero-day vulnerabilities (undocumented flaws unknown to defenders)
Nation-state actors and advanced persistent threat groups

slides.footer: AP TIP: If the attacker ran a purchased tool against a known flaw, that is low-skilled. If they exploited an undocumented vulnerability or customized the attack, that is high-skilled.
slides.script: Two categories. Low-skilled adversaries rely on tools other people built. They download exploit kits, point them at known weaknesses, and run them. If those known vulnerabilities are patched, they are stuck. High-skilled adversaries can build their own tools and adapt them. The critical marker for high-skilled is zero-days: vulnerabilities that have NOT been publicly documented yet, so defenders have no patch. If the AP exam describes an attacker using an undocumented flaw or creating a custom payload, that is high-skilled. If they ran a purchased tool against a known CVE, that is low-skilled.

slides.heading: Six Adversary Motivations (EK 1.3.A.2)
bullets: Greed — financial gain through theft, ransomware, or fraud
Desire for recognition — proving skill or gaining notoriety in hacking communities
Dedication to a cause — hacktivism targeting organizations tied to a political or social goal
Revenge — targeting a former employer, competitor, or individual
Politics — state-sponsored attacks advancing national interests or disrupting adversaries
Beliefs — ideological motivation to expose, disrupt, or destroy perceived wrongdoing
slides.script: EK 1.3.A.2 lists exactly six motivations. Memorize all six: greed, recognition, dedication to a cause, revenge, politics, beliefs. The AP exam can give you a scenario and ask you to identify the most likely motivation. A hacker leaking corporate secrets to protest environmental damage is dediction to a cause. A hacker demanding a ransom payment is greed. A government operation targeting foreign infrastructure is politics. Skill level and motivation together let you build a full picture of the adversary. Write all six in your Guided Notes.

slides.label: Three Wireless Attacks
slides.kicker: Evil Twin · Jamming · War Driving · Learning Objective 1.3.B
slides.script: Section two. Three wireless attacks, and the CB framework names all three explicitly. The AP exam will describe one of them and ask you to identify which attack it is. The descriptions are distinct enough that if you know the mechanism of each one, you can always pick the right answer. Ten minutes.

slides.heading: Three CB-Specified Wireless Attacks
slides.subheading: The AP exam names all three. Know the mechanism, not just the name.

items.title: Evil Twin Attack
items.summary: Adversary sets up a WAP with an SSID matching a real network. Victims connect unknowingly and the adversary captures their traffic. HTTPS limits eavesdropping on content, but NOT credential harvesting via a fake login form.

items.title: Jamming / DoS Attack
items.summary: Adversary floods an area with a strong EM signal on the same frequency as the target network, so legitimate users cannot reach the access point. The CB framework classifies this explicitly as a denial-of-service (DoS) attack.

items.title: War Driving Attack
items.summary: Adversary detects wireless network beacons while driving or walking an area, mapping network types and where signals extend beyond the building perimeter. This is reconnaissance, not access.

slides.script: Three attacks. Fill in the attack table in your Guided Notes as we go through these. First: evil twin. The adversary sets up their own WAP with an SSID that looks like the real network. You connect to the attacker's AP thinking it is the coffee shop Wi-Fi. Now all your traffic flows through their equipment. Second: jamming. The adversary floods the wireless frequency with electromagnetic interference. Your device can hear the AP but the signal is overwhelmed. Nobody on that network can communicate. Third: war driving. The adversary drives or walks through an area detecting which wireless networks are broadcasting and where the signal bleeds outside the building. This is pure reconnaissance. They are not inside your network yet, they are mapping the attack surface.

slides.heading: The HTTPS Myth
slides.misconception: If I use HTTPS, an evil twin cannot hurt me.
slides.whyItHappens: EK 1.3.B.1 says the adversary cannot read traffic that uses an encrypted protocol like HTTPS. Students read this and assume they are fully protected from evil twin attacks as long as they see the padlock.
slides.correction: HTTPS defeats eavesdropping on content. It does NOT stop credential harvesting. If you submit your username and password to a fake login form, you are handing them to the attacker regardless of whether the form is served over HTTPS.
slides.example: An adversary's fake captive portal can have a valid SSL certificate. You see HTTPS in the address bar, you log in with your Google account — and HTTPS faithfully encrypts the traffic straight to the attacker's server.
slides.script: This is the most common misconception I see on this topic, and the CB framework practically guarantees it will show up on the exam. Yes, EK 1.3.B.1 says HTTPS defeats evil twin eavesdropping. That is true. If your traffic is HTTPS-encrypted, the attacker cannot read the content of your sessions. But here is what students miss: HTTPS does nothing when you willingly type your password into a fake form. The adversary does not need to intercept traffic. You gave them the credentials yourself. Always ask: am I logging into anything? If yes, HTTPS on the connection means nothing if the destination is fake.

slides.heading: Active vs Passive Interception
slides.subheading: Enrichment: not directly CB-required, but it helps you distinguish attack mechanisms in complex scenarios.

left.title: Packet Sniffing
examples: Listens to traffic on the same network segment
Does NOT modify or redirect traffic
HTTPS defeats it — encrypted content is unreadable
Tool example: Wireshark on a NIC in monitor mode

right.title: Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)
right.definition: Actively sits between victim and destination
examples: Can modify traffic in transit, not just observe it
An evil twin IS a form of MITM — the attacker controls the AP
Can serve fake pages, inject content, or redirect requests
Active attacks are harder to execute but more powerful

slides.footer: AP TIP: All evil twins are MITM attacks; not all MITM attacks are evil twins. The CB exam tests the specific attack name, not the general category.
slides.script: Enrichment track only. Packet sniffing is passive. You are sitting on a network and listening to what other devices broadcast. You observe, you do not touch. HTTPS defeats this completely because the content is encrypted. MITM is active. You position yourself between the victim and the destination and can modify what they see and send. An evil twin is a specific form of MITM where the attacker controls the access point itself. That is a more powerful position than just sniffing. When the CB exam describes an evil twin, call it an evil twin, not MITM. Both are accurate, but the exam rewards precision.

slides.label: Three CB Defenses
slides.kicker: SSID Verification · VPN · Protocol Awareness · Learning Objective 1.3.C
slides.script: Section three. The three defenses. LO 1.3.C says you need to be able to describe actions individuals can take to protect themselves. The CB framework specifies exactly three. Know all three, know their limitations, and know which one stops which attack.

slides.heading: Three CB-Required Protections
slides.subheading: EKs 1.3.C.1, 1.3.C.2, and 1.3.C.3 each name a specific action. Know the limitation of each one.

items.title: Verify the Network Name Exactly
items.summary: Before joining, confirm the SSID exactly matches the network you intend to join. A single-character difference can signal an evil twin. This is the direct defense against EK 1.3.B.1.

items.title: Use a Virtual Private Network
items.summary: Encrypts all traffic from your device to the VPN operator's system, preventing a local attacker from reading it. CRITICAL: the VPN provider can still view the traffic — a VPN shifts trust, it does not eliminate it.

items.title: Consider Protocol Encryption
items.summary: Most modern internet protocols are encrypted, but some data (e.g., DNS queries) can leak even on HTTPS connections. Consider whether your activity is sensitive enough to avoid unencrypted Wi-Fi entirely.

slides.script: Three defenses. First: verify the SSID exactly. Before you connect to anything, look at the name character by character. Coffee shop posted a sign? Check it against the SSID. One extra space, a zero for an O, a lowercase L for a capital I -- those are evil twin tells. Second: VPN. VPN encrypts your traffic from your device to the VPN company's server. A local attacker on the same network cannot read it. BUT -- and this is in the EK verbatim -- the VPN provider can view the traffic. You shifted trust from the coffee shop Wi-Fi to the VPN company. You did not eliminate trust. Third: protocol encryption awareness. This one is about knowing that not all your activity is equally risky. DNS queries in particular can leak even when you are on HTTPS sites. If the data is sensitive, consider whether the network is worth the risk at all.

slides.heading: VPN ≠ Invisible + Safe
slides.misconception: Using a VPN makes me anonymous and completely safe on public Wi-Fi.
slides.whyItHappens: VPN marketing promises 'military-grade encryption' and 'complete privacy.' Students interpret this as total protection from all threats on any network.
slides.correction: Per EK 1.3.C.1, the VPN provider can view the traffic. A VPN shifts trust from the Wi-Fi operator to the VPN company. It does not hide your account activity from websites, and it provides zero protection if you submit credentials to a phishing site.
slides.example: You use a VPN at a coffee shop and log into a phishing site mimicking your bank. The VPN encrypted the traffic — and delivered your credentials to the attacker perfectly.
slides.script: VPNs are not magic. They are a trust-shifting tool. Without a VPN on public Wi-Fi, you trust the Wi-Fi operator and everyone else on that network not to sniff your traffic. With a VPN, you remove the Wi-Fi threat but you are now trusting the VPN company with everything. EK 1.3.C.1 is explicit about this. On the AP exam, any answer choice that says 'VPN protects you completely' or 'VPN makes you anonymous' is wrong. Look for that on the distractors. The correct framing is always: VPN reduces exposure to local attackers while shifting trust to the VPN provider.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 1.3.A–C)

terms.definition: Hardware device that creates a wireless network, allowing devices to connect via Wi-Fi (EK 1.3.B.1).
The name of a wireless network that devices display when scanning for available connections (EK 1.3.B.1).
An adversary's rogue WAP mimicking a legitimate network's SSID to intercept victim traffic (EK 1.3.B.1).
Flooding a wireless frequency with EM interference to prevent legitimate network communication; a form of DoS (EK 1.3.B.2).
Any attack that prevents legitimate users from accessing a resource; jamming is a wireless DoS (EK 1.3.B.2).
Detecting wireless network beacons while moving through an area to map networks and find signal leakage (EK 1.3.B.3).
Encrypts traffic from your device to the VPN operator's server; shifts, but does not eliminate, trust (EK 1.3.C.1).
Encrypted web protocol that defeats eavesdropping on content but does not protect against fake login forms (EK 1.3.C.2).
Uses pre-built tools purchased online and exploits known, documented vulnerabilities; cannot adapt tools (EK 1.3.A.1).
Creates or modifies their own tools and can discover zero-day vulnerabilities not yet known to defenders (EK 1.3.A.1).

slides.script: Ten terms. Copy any you do not already have locked in. The ones the AP exam tests most directly are evil twin, SSID, VPN, and the two adversary skill levels. Zero-day is also worth memorizing cold: it means an undocumented vulnerability that defenders have no patch for, which is the defining capability of a high-skilled adversary. If you see zero-day in an AP scenario, classify the attacker as high-skilled.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: These two questions map to LO 1.3.B and LO 1.3.C. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: Describe an evil twin attack. Identify one specific thing HTTPS prevents an evil twin attacker from doing, and one thing HTTPS does NOT prevent. Reference EK 1.3.B.1 in your answer.
A student says using a VPN at a coffee shop makes them completely safe. Identify one way the VPN does protect them, and one way the VPN does NOT protect them. Use EK 1.3.C.1 in your response.
slides.directions: Independent. 7 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Pencils down on everything else. Seven minutes. Two questions. Both are LO-mapped and both types have appeared on released AP Cybersecurity materials. Question one: describe the evil twin and give me the specific HTTPS limitation from EK 1.3.B.1. I do not want a general answer. I want the exact mechanism. Question two: quote the EK 1.3.C.1 limitation on VPNs in your response. The answer is in the essential knowledge, so if you have it in your Guided Notes, you can use it. Go.

slides.eyebrow: Your Turn — It’s All on the Site

items.tail: Read a scenario and name the attack — today’s skill.

slides.script: Everything from class is on the site. Start on the Lesson 1.3 page and run the self-check, then the wireless-attack practice where you name the attack in each scenario. Write the lesson page URL down now.

slides.heading: End of Day 1
todayPoints: Classified adversaries by skill level (low vs high) and memorized six CB-listed motivations (LO 1.3.A)
Identified three CB-specified wireless attacks: evil twin, jamming/DoS, and war driving (LO 1.3.B)
Learned three CB-required defenses and their critical limitations (LO 1.3.C)
Busted two high-frequency misconceptions about HTTPS and VPN coverage
nextPoints: Analyze four real Wi-Fi incidents as a security analyst using the Wireless Attack Identifier
Classify each scenario with the CB attack framework and name the defense that stops it
Practice the predict-first strategy on AP-style wireless attack questions
slides.teaser: Next class you stop being the student and become the analyst: four real Wi-Fi incidents, and for each one you name the attack and the defense that would have stopped it.
slides.script: Wrap up. Today you classified adversaries, learned three CB-required attacks and three CB-required defenses, and worked through the two misconceptions that trip up the most students on this topic. Next class you apply all of it. Four real Wi-Fi incidents, classify the attack in each one, name the defense that would have stopped it. You are going to work through those like a security analyst, not like a student guessing at answers. Before you leave, hand in your Guided Notes. And think about the bell ringer: the network name alone was your entire decision-making process. That is exactly what an evil twin exploits.

quiz.title: Lesson 1.3 — Best Practices for Public Networks — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 9 questions, 20 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: An adversary buys a ready-made hacking tool online and uses it to exploit a vulnerability that has been publicly documented for months. The adversary cannot write or modify the tool themselves. How does the CB framework classify this adversary?
options: Low-skilled — relies on tools created by others that exploit known vulnerabilities
High-skilled — creates new tools and discovers undocumented zero-day vulnerabilities
Low-skilled — discovers undocumented zero-day vulnerabilities without using any tools
High-skilled — purchases finished tools that only ever target publicly known flaws
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.A.1 says low-skilled adversaries rely on malicious cyber tools created by others that can be purchased online, and those tools exploit known vulnerabilities — exactly this adversary. Option B describes a high-skilled adversary. Options C and D scramble the skill-level traits, pairing the wrong capabilities with each label.

questions.stem: According to the CB framework, what distinguishes a high-skilled adversary from a low-skilled one?
options: They can only purchase tools that exploit vulnerabilities already documented publicly
They can create or modify malicious tools and discover undocumented zero-day vulnerabilities
They are motivated solely by greed and never by recognition, politics, or beliefs
They jam wireless signals while low-skilled adversaries set up evil twin access points
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.A.1 states high-skilled adversaries can create new tools or modify existing ones and can discover undocumented vulnerabilities, known as zero days. Option A describes the low-skilled adversary. Option C wrongly narrows motivation, which EK 1.3.A.2 lists broadly. Option D invents a skill-to-attack mapping the CED does not make.

questions.stem: A threat report notes that an attacker group is driven by a mix of revenge, dedication to a cause, and desire for recognition. Which CB-specified idea does this directly illustrate?
options: That adversaries are classified only by their technical skill level
That encrypted protocols like HTTPS protect traffic from an evil twin
That adversaries have a variety of motivations behind their attacks
That jamming attacks are a form of denial-of-service attack
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.A.2 lists motivations including greed, desire for recognition, dedication to a cause, revenge, politics, or beliefs — the report names several of these. Option A addresses skill (EK 1.3.A.1), not motivation. Options B and D are true CED statements but concern wireless attacks, not adversary motivation.

questions.stem: At a coffee shop, a victim connects to a wireless access point whose SSID is identical to the shop's real network but is actually run by an adversary, who then captures the victim's traffic. The victim's banking session over HTTPS stays unreadable to the attacker. Which attack is this, and why is the banking traffic safe?
options: War driving — the adversary detected the beacon while walking past the building
Evil twin — a rogue access point imitates the SSID, yet HTTPS encryption hides that traffic
Jamming — a strong EM signal blocks the access point, though HTTPS still functions normally
Evil twin — the rogue access point can read all traffic, including the encrypted HTTPS session
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.B.1 describes an evil twin as a rogue WAP using an SSID similar or identical to the target so victims unknowingly connect and the adversary captures traffic — but the adversary cannot read traffic using an encrypted protocol like HTTPS. Option D names the attack right but contradicts the encryption point. Options A and C name different attacks.

questions.stem: A wireless network suddenly becomes unusable because an adversary floods the area with a strong electromagnetic signal in the network's frequency range, preventing legitimate traffic between the access point and users. Under the CB framework, this jamming attack is also an example of what?
options: A zero-day exploit against an undocumented vulnerability
An evil twin attack using a duplicated service set identifier
A war driving attack conducted from a moving vehicle
A denial-of-service attack that blocks access to resources
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.B.2 says jamming floods an area with a strong EM signal in the network's frequency range, preventing legitimate traffic, and that this attack preventing users from accessing resources is called a denial-of-service (DoS) attack. Options A, B, and C name unrelated concepts or different wireless attacks.

questions.stem: In the CB framework, what does an adversary attempt to do during a war driving attack?
options: Flood the frequency range with electromagnetic noise to block legitimate traffic
Stand up a rogue access point with an SSID identical to a target network
Detect wireless network beacons while driving or walking around a target
Decrypt HTTPS sessions captured from victims on a public network
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.B.3 says in a war driving attack adversaries try to detect wireless network beacons while driving or walking around a target, then gather information and find where the signal extends outside the building. Option A is jamming (EK 1.3.B.2), option B is an evil twin (EK 1.3.B.1), and option D contradicts EK 1.3.B.1.

questions.stem: Before joining the airport Wi-Fi, a traveler carefully compares the network name shown on their device against the official name posted at the help desk to make sure they match exactly. Which CB-specified protective action is the traveler taking?
options: Verifying that the network name exactly matches the network they intend to join
Using a VPN so that all of their traffic is encrypted to the VPN operator
Avoiding unencrypted networks to protect data such as their DNS queries
Relying on HTTPS to keep an evil twin from reading their network traffic
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.C.1 says individuals should verify that the name of any wireless network they join exactly matches the name of the network they intend to join — precisely the matching check described. Options B, C, and D are other real CED defenses (EK 1.3.C.3, 1.3.C.2, 1.3.B.1) but not the action shown here.

questions.stem: A user worried about sensitive data on public Wi-Fi installs a VPN. According to the CB framework, what is the key limitation they should understand about this choice?
options: The VPN leaves DNS queries fully exposed to every nearby wireless user
The VPN provider itself can view the user's traffic even though the service provider cannot
The VPN only works on networks whose name matches the intended network exactly
The VPN converts every jamming attack into a denial-of-service attack automatically
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.C.3 says a VPN encrypts all traffic to the VPN operator's system, and although this prevents a service provider from viewing traffic, the VPN provider can view the traffic. Options A, C, and D misstate the CED or attach unrelated concepts to the VPN.

questions.stem: The CB framework notes that most internet protocols are encrypted, yet still advises individuals to weigh the sensitivity of their data before joining unencrypted Wi-Fi. What example of vulnerable data does the CED give?
options: Saved passwords stored inside the device's local keychain
Cloned voice samples scraped from social-media videos
DNS queries that may not be protected on an unencrypted network
Beacon frames broadcast by the wireless access point
questions.rationale: EK 1.3.C.2 says most internet protocols are encrypted, but individuals may consider the sensitivity of their data when choosing whether to join unencrypted Wi-Fi to protect vulnerable data such as DNS queries. Options A, B, and D are not the example the CED gives here.
